walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Star Boards
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Azul: Summer Pavilion. Let's build a stunning Portuguese pavilion together. First, give each player a star board. This is your personal player board showing six colorful stars arranged in a hexagonal pattern. Each star has numbered spaces from one through six, and each star requires tiles of a specific color. Take a moment to look at the board and notice how the stars overlap at their edges.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Tile Bag
walkthrough.text: Find all 132 tiles in the box. There are six colors with 22 tiles each. Drop every single tile into the cloth bag and give it a good shake. This bag is your supply for the entire game. You will draw from it to fill the factory displays each round.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Factory Displays
walkthrough.text: Place the round factory displays in the center of the table. For two players use five factories, for three players use seven, and for four players use nine. Leave space in the middle between them for the center of the table area where overflow tiles will collect during drafting.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Supply Spaces
walkthrough.text: Each player board has a supply area along the bottom. This is where you store tiles between the drafting phase and the placement phase. You will also see a score track running around the edge of your board. Place your scoring marker on zero.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Bonus Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the pillar, statue, and window bonus tokens. Place them near the main play area where everyone can reach them. These are the surrounding bonuses you earn by completing all spaces around certain spots on your star board. They let you draw extra tiles from the bag as a reward.

walkthrough.title: Note the Wild Color for Round One
walkthrough.text: Each of the six rounds has a different wild color. The wild color for round one is marked on the round tracker. Wild tiles are special because you can pick them up as a bonus when drafting, and they substitute for any color when placing tiles. Make sure everyone knows which color is wild before you begin.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Factories
walkthrough.text: Draw four tiles from the bag and place them on each factory display. Do this for every factory on the table. These tiles are now available for drafting. Once all factories are filled, you are ready to start round one.

walkthrough.title: Determine the First Player
walkthrough.text: The player who most recently visited Portugal goes first. If nobody has, choose randomly. Play proceeds clockwise. Let's begin drafting tiles for your Summer Pavilion.

bullets: Each player takes 1 star board

Place scoring marker on 0

bullets: 132 tiles total — 6 colors, 22 each
Place all tiles in the cloth bag and mix

bullets: 2P → 5 factories, 3P → 7 factories, 4P → 9 factories
Leave center area open for overflow tiles

bullets: Supply area on player board stores drafted tiles
Score track around the edge — start at 0

bullets: Pillar, statue, and window bonus tokens placed centrally
Earned by surrounding specific board positions

bullets: Each round has a different wild color (rotates through all 6)
Wild tiles act as substitutes when placing

summary.title: Fill the Factories
bullets: Draw 4 tiles from bag onto each factory display
All factories filled → ready to draft

summary.title: Determine the First Player
bullets: Most recent Portugal visitor goes first (or random)
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: Game Structure
walkthrough.text: Azul: Summer Pavilion is played over six rounds. Each round has three phases: Acquire Tiles, Play Tiles, and Prepare Next Round. Let's walk through each phase so you know exactly what to do on your turn.

walkthrough.title: Acquire Tiles — Choose a Factory or Center
walkthrough.text: Choose all tiles of one non-wild color from that location and add them to your supply. If there are any tiles of the current wild color at that same location, you may also take one wild tile as a bonus.

walkthrough.title: Acquire Tiles — Leftovers Go to the Center
walkthrough.text: When you take tiles from a factory display, any remaining tiles on that factory get pushed to the center of the table. The center grows throughout the round as more factories are emptied. When someone drafts from the center, they take all tiles of one non-wild color from the entire center pile, plus one optional wild tile.

walkthrough.title: Acquire Tiles — Round Ends
walkthrough.text: Players keep taking turns drafting tiles until all factory displays and the center are completely empty. Once every tile has been claimed, the Acquire phase ends and everyone moves to the Play Tiles phase simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Play Tiles — Placing on Your Star Board
walkthrough.text: To place a tile on a numbered space, you must pay a cost in tiles equal to that space's number. For example, placing on a three space costs three tiles of the matching color. One tile goes onto the space and the rest go back to the bag. Wild tiles can substitute for any color when paying the cost.

walkthrough.title: Play Tiles — Scoring Each Placement
walkthrough.text: Every time you place a tile, you score points equal to the size of the connected group it joins. A tile placed next to two already-filled spaces scores three points because the group is now three tiles. An isolated tile with no neighbors scores just one point. Connections count horizontally and vertically within the same star.

walkthrough.title: Play Tiles — Surrounding Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Certain spaces on your board are surrounded by pillar, statue, or window icons. When you fill all the spaces around one of these icons, you immediately earn bonus tile draws from the bag. Pillars award one to four bonus tiles, statues award two to eight, and windows award three to twelve. You keep the drawn tiles in your supply for future rounds.

walkthrough.title: Play Tiles — Keep Maximum Four
walkthrough.text: After placing all the tiles you want, check your supply. You may keep a maximum of four tiles between rounds. Any tiles beyond four must be discarded back into the bag. Choose carefully which four tiles to save because they carry over to next round's placement phase.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Next Round
walkthrough.text: Advance the round marker to reveal the new wild color. Refill all factory displays by drawing four tiles from the bag onto each one. The new round begins with a fresh Acquire phase. The wild color changes every round, cycling through all six colors over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Scoring — Star Bonuses
walkthrough.text: After round six, the game ends and final scoring begins. First, check each star on your board. If you have completely filled all six spaces of a star, you earn a large bonus. Each completed star is worth a significant point boost on top of what you scored during placement.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Scoring — Number Set Bonus
walkthrough.text: Next, check each number across all six stars. If you have covered the same number in every star, you earn the number set bonus. For example, if you filled the one space in all six stars, that is a complete set. Filling all six of a particular number earns bonus points. The highest total score wins.

summary.title: Game Structure
bullets: 6 rounds, each with 3 phases
Acquire Tiles → Play Tiles → Prepare Next Round

Highest total score wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Round
walkthrough.text: Let's play through the first two rounds of a three-player game together. We have Alex, Blake, and Casey sitting around the table with seven factory displays loaded with tiles. The wild color for round one is purple. By the end of this tutorial, you will understand drafting, tile placement costs, surrounding bonuses, and the four-tile carry limit.

walkthrough.title: Alex Drafts First
walkthrough.text: Alex looks at the seven factories and spots one with three blue tiles and one red tile. Let's take all three blue tiles from that factory. The leftover red tile slides to the center of the table. Alex also notices a purple wild tile on that factory, so they grab it too. Alex now has three blue tiles and one wild tile in their supply.

walkthrough.title: Blake Gets Greedy
walkthrough.text: Blake sees the center already has one red tile, and two factories each have red tiles on them. Blake wants to wait and draft a huge haul from the center later. But other players keep pushing non-red tiles to the center too. By the time Blake finally drafts from the center, they collect seven red tiles and one wild tile. That is a massive haul, but Blake only needs four reds for the placement they planned.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Over-Drafting and Losing Tiles
walkthrough.text: Blake placed one tile on a four space, using four reds. That leaves three reds and one wild in their supply. But wait, Blake also had two orange tiles from an earlier draft. That is six tiles total, and you can only keep four between rounds. Blake must discard two tiles back to the bag. Those tiles are wasted and could have gone to a future placement.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Draft What You Can Use
walkthrough.text: Let's learn from Blake's mistake. Before drafting a large batch, count how many tiles you can actually place this round plus the four you can keep. Drafting more than you can use means tiles go back to the bag for free. Plan your drafts around your placement goals, not just grabbing the biggest pile available.

walkthrough.title: Casey Places on the Five Space
walkthrough.text: Casey collected five orange tiles over two drafts and decides to place on the five space of the orange star. That costs five tiles. One goes onto the board and four go back into the bag. Casey scores one point because the tile is isolated with no neighbors. That is a lot of tiles spent for a single point right now.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Expensive Spaces Without Enough Payoff
walkthrough.text: Casey paid five tiles to place on the five space but scored only one point because there were no adjacent tiles. Let's think about this differently. If Casey had placed on the two space instead, it would cost only two tiles and still score one point. Casey could then use the remaining three tiles for another placement somewhere else, scoring additional points.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Start Small and Build Groups
walkthrough.text: Let's remember this for our own games. High-numbered spaces are expensive, so they pay off best when placed next to existing tiles where the connected group bonus multiplies your score. Start with cheaper spaces like ones, twos, and threes to establish groups. Then place expensive tiles adjacent to those groups so the group-size scoring rewards the investment.

walkthrough.title: Round Two — Alex Builds Smartly
walkthrough.text: Round two begins with a new wild color. Alex already has two blue tiles placed next to each other on their star board from round one. Now Alex places a third blue tile adjacent to them, paying the cost of three tiles for the three space. This tile joins the group of two, making a group of three. Alex scores three points for this single placement.

walkthrough.title: Alex Triggers a Pillar Bonus
walkthrough.text: Let's look at Alex's board more carefully. That third tile just filled the last space surrounding a pillar icon. Alex earns a pillar bonus and draws one bonus tile from the bag. This bonus tile goes straight into Alex's supply for use in future placements. Surrounding bonuses are powerful because they give you free tiles without spending a draft action.

walkthrough.title: Blake Ignores a Statue Opportunity
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, Blake has four tiles placed around a statue icon but leaves the fifth space empty. Blake focuses on a different star instead, chasing a big group elsewhere. The statue would have awarded bonus tile draws, but Blake does not notice the setup. That statue sits there for the rest of the game, one tile away from triggering.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Surrounding Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what Blake missed. The statue bonus would have awarded several bonus tiles drawn from the bag. Those free tiles could fuel future placements without costing any draft actions. Blake was so focused on building one large group that they ignored the bonus sitting right there on their board. One tile away from a huge reward, and they walked right past it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check Your Bonus Icons
walkthrough.text: Let's make this a habit. Before choosing where to place tiles each round, scan your board for pillar, statue, and window icons. Count how many surrounding spaces are already filled. If you are one or two tiles away from triggering a bonus, prioritize completing it. The bonus tiles you draw compound into more placements and more points over the remaining rounds.

walkthrough.title: Checking the Scores
walkthrough.text: After two rounds, Alex has 11 points from smart group building and a pillar bonus. Casey has five points after the costly five-space placement and a more efficient second-round play. Blake has eight points but missed the statue bonus that could have pushed them ahead. The lesson is clear: draft efficiently, build groups from small to large, and never ignore your surrounding bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen the three most common beginner mistakes in Azul: Summer Pavilion. Over-drafting wastes tiles at the end of a round. Placing on expensive spaces without adjacent tiles wastes your investment. Ignoring surrounding bonuses leaves free tiles on the table. Keep these lessons in mind and you will build a stunning pavilion.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are two things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for drafting and placement advice that will sharpen your strategy.

Second — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Enjoy building your pavilion.

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → drafting and placement advice
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Draft with a Plan
walkthrough.text: Before you take tiles from a factory or the center, know exactly where they will go on your board. Count the cost of the space you are targeting and check whether you already have tiles of that color in your supply. Let's avoid grabbing tiles just because there are a lot of them. Every draft should serve a specific placement goal.

walkthrough.title: Build Connected Groups Early
walkthrough.text: Scoring is based on connected group size, so let's place tiles next to each other whenever possible. Start with low-cost spaces like the one and two spaces to establish footholds in each star. Then expand outward by filling adjacent spaces. A group of four connected tiles means every new tile added to that group scores at least five points.

walkthrough.title: Use Wild Tiles Wisely
walkthrough.text: Wild tiles are incredibly flexible because they substitute for any color when paying placement costs. Let's save them for expensive placements where you are one tile short of the required cost. Do not waste wilds on cheap one-cost or two-cost spaces where they are not needed. A well-timed wild tile can unlock a five or six space that would otherwise be out of reach.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Surrounding Bonuses
walkthrough.text: Pillar, statue, and window bonuses award free tiles drawn from the bag. Let's scan our board each round and identify which bonus icons are closest to being completed. Completing a bonus early in the game gives you extra tiles that compound over multiple rounds. A statue bonus in round two is worth far more than the same bonus in round six because you have more rounds to use the tiles.

walkthrough.title: Respect the Four-Tile Carry Limit
walkthrough.text: You can only keep four tiles between rounds, so let's plan accordingly. If you have more tiles in your supply than you can place plus four, you will waste the excess. Draft in amounts you can actually use. When choosing which four tiles to keep, prioritize colors that set up next round's placements or colors matching the upcoming wild color.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Wild Color Rotation
walkthrough.text: Each round features a different wild color. Let's pay attention to which color is wild next round. If you know green is wild next round, carrying green tiles into that round is less valuable because you will be able to draft greens as wild bonuses. Instead, carry tiles of colors you actually need and let the wild color supplement your drafts naturally.

walkthrough.title: Track Opponents in Later Rounds
walkthrough.text: In the mid and late game, let's start watching what colors opponents are drafting heavily. If two players both want blue, the supply dries up fast. Pivot to less contested colors when you can. Also watch the center pile. Sometimes letting it grow and drafting a moderate batch from the center is more efficient than fighting over factories.

walkthrough.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
walkthrough.text: Let's avoid these three traps. First, filling expensive spaces before establishing groups means you pay high costs for minimal points. Second, hoarding tiles past the four-tile limit wastes your drafts. Third, ignoring surrounding bonuses leaves free tiles on the table that your opponents are gladly collecting. Fix these three habits and your scores will jump immediately.

summary.title: Draft with a Plan
bullets: Know your target space before drafting
Count the cost and check existing supply
Every draft should serve a specific placement goal

summary.title: Build Connected Groups Early
bullets: Score = connected group size per placement
Start with 1-cost and 2-cost spaces → build outward
Group of 4 = every new adjacent tile scores 5+ pts

summary.title: Use Wild Tiles Wisely
bullets: Wild tiles substitute for any color when placing
Save wilds for expensive 5-cost and 6-cost spaces
Don't waste wilds on cheap 1-2 cost placements

summary.title: Prioritize Surrounding Bonuses
bullets: Pillars/statues/windows = free tiles from bag
Complete bonuses early → more rounds to use the tiles
Scan board each round for near-complete bonus icons

summary.title: Respect the Four-Tile Carry Limit
bullets: Max 4 tiles kept between rounds — excess discarded
Draft only what you can place + 4 carry
Keep tiles that set up next round's placements

summary.title: Watch the Wild Color Rotation
bullets: Each round has a different wild color
Don't carry tiles matching next round's wild — you'll draft those for free
Carry colors you need, let wilds supplement

summary.title: Track Opponents in Later Rounds
bullets: Watch which colors opponents draft heavily → pivot to less contested colors
Center pile can offer efficient moderate drafts
Avoid fighting over scarce colors when alternatives exist

summary.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
bullets: Expensive spaces before groups = high cost, low score
Hoarding past 4-tile limit = wasted drafts
Ignoring surrounding bonuses = free tiles left on table

walkthrough.title: Wild Color Exploitation
walkthrough.text: The wild color changes each round, and savvy players plan around it. Let's draft the wild color aggressively because every wild tile you collect is a flexible placement tool. When the wild color matches a star you are building, you get double value: those tiles count as their own color for that star and as wildcards elsewhere. Time your big pushes in specific stars to coincide with their color being wild.

walkthrough.title: Bonus Chaining
walkthrough.text: Surrounding bonuses draw tiles from the bag, and those drawn tiles can sometimes complete another placement that triggers yet another bonus. Let's set up chain reactions by positioning our placements so that a bonus draw could fuel the next bonus completion in the same round. This requires planning two to three placements ahead, but the payoff is enormous. A single chain can net you six or more bonus tiles in one round.

walkthrough.title: Star Completion Planning
walkthrough.text: Completing an entire star earns a large end-game bonus. Let's identify one or two stars to complete fully and work toward them from round one. Each star requires tiles of a specific color, so make sure you are drafting that color consistently across multiple rounds. Completing a star also requires filling all six numbered spaces, meaning you need a total of 21 tiles of that color over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Number Set Strategy
walkthrough.text: The number set bonus rewards filling the same number across all six stars. Let's focus on low numbers like one and two because they cost fewer tiles and appear in every star. Filling all six one-spaces costs only six tiles total and earns the number set bonus. Compare that to filling all six five-spaces, which costs 30 tiles. Low-number sets are far more efficient and leave resources for other objectives.

walkthrough.title: Deny and Draft
walkthrough.text: In a tight game, let's pay attention to what opponents need. If an opponent is one tile away from completing a star or triggering a window bonus, consider drafting tiles they need even if those tiles are not your top priority. Denial drafting costs you very little when the tiles you take are still usable on your board. The key is recognizing when an opponent's scoring opportunity is worth more than your alternative draft.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Tile Efficiency
walkthrough.text: In rounds five and six, every tile matters. Let's maximize efficiency by placing tiles that score the most points per tile spent. A two-cost placement that joins a group of five scores six points for two tiles. A six-cost placement in isolation scores one point for six tiles. In the final rounds, chase group-building placements over expensive isolated spaces. The math always favors adjacency over ambition.

walkthrough.title: Balancing Short-Term and Long-Term Goals
walkthrough.text: Let's find the right balance between scoring points now and setting up end-game bonuses. A player who chases only group-size scoring in the early rounds may have a lead at halftime but lose to someone who completed two stars and three number sets. Conversely, someone who only builds toward end-game bonuses may fall too far behind to recover. The strongest strategy is to pursue surrounding bonuses and group scoring in rounds one through three, then shift focus to completing stars and number sets in rounds four through six.

summary.title: Wild Color Exploitation
bullets: Draft wild color aggressively — max flexibility
Time star pushes to coincide with that star's color being wild
Wild tiles = double value when they match your target star

summary.title: Bonus Chaining
bullets: Set up placements so bonus draws fuel the next bonus
Plan 2-3 placements ahead for chain reactions
A single chain can net 6+ bonus tiles in one round

summary.title: Star Completion Planning
bullets: Target 1-2 stars to complete fully for end-game bonus
Each star needs 21 tiles total (1+2+3+4+5+6) of its color
Draft that color consistently across multiple rounds

summary.title: Number Set Strategy
bullets: Fill same number across all 6 stars → number set bonus
Low numbers (1s, 2s) cost far fewer tiles — more efficient
All six 1-spaces = 6 tiles; all six 5-spaces = 30 tiles

summary.title: Deny and Draft
bullets: Draft tiles opponents need when it doesn't hurt you
Denial is strongest when opponent is 1 tile from a big bonus
Only deny when the tiles are still usable on your board

summary.title: Late-Game Tile Efficiency
bullets: Rounds 5-6: maximize pts per tile spent
2-cost placement joining group of 5 = 6 pts for 2 tiles
6-cost isolated = 1 pt for 6 tiles — always favor adjacency

summary.title: Balancing Short-Term and Long-Term Goals
bullets: Rounds 1-3: focus on surrounding bonuses + group scoring
Rounds 4-6: shift to completing stars + number sets
Pure end-game or pure early-game strategies both lose to balance

entries.definition: One of 132 diamond-shaped pieces in six colors (22 per color). Tiles are drafted from factory displays and the center, then placed on star board spaces. Unused tiles are returned to the bag or discarded at round end.
Cloth bag holding all 132 tiles at game start. Tiles are drawn from the bag to fill factory displays at the beginning of each round and as bonus draws from surrounding bonuses.

Circular platform holding 4 randomly drawn tiles. Players draft all tiles of one non-wild color from a factory. Leftover tiles are pushed to the center. Player count determines number of factories: 5 (2P), 7 (3P), or 9 (4P).
Personal player board showing 6 colored stars arranged in a hexagonal pattern. Each star has spaces numbered 1-6 requiring tiles of a specific color. Stars overlap at edges, sharing certain spaces.
The area on your player board where drafted tiles are stored before placement. Tiles in the supply carry between the Acquire and Play phases. Maximum 4 tiles may remain in the supply between rounds.
The act of selecting tiles during the Acquire phase. Choose all tiles of one non-wild color from a single factory display or the center. You may also take one wild tile from the same location.

Open area where leftover factory tiles accumulate. When drafting from the center, take all tiles of one non-wild color plus optionally one wild tile. The center can grow large in later turns of a round.
A numbered position (1-6) within a colored star on the player board. The number indicates the placement cost in tiles. One tile fills the space; the remaining cost tiles are returned to the bag.

The number of tiles required to fill a star space. A space numbered 4 costs 4 tiles: 1 placed on the board, 3 returned to the bag. Wild tiles can substitute for the required color when paying the cost.
A set of adjacent filled spaces within the same star. When placing a tile, score points equal to the total size of the connected group it creates or joins. Larger groups score more per placement.

Each round, one of the six colors is designated wild. Wild tiles can substitute for any color when paying placement costs. During drafting, you may take one wild tile as a bonus alongside your chosen non-wild color.

The wild color changes each round, cycling through all 6 colors over the 6-round game. The current wild color is shown on the round tracker. Planning around upcoming wild colors is a key strategic element.
Triggered when all spaces surrounding a pillar icon on the board are filled. Awards 1-4 bonus tile draws from the bag depending on the specific pillar. Drawn tiles go to your supply.
Triggered when all spaces surrounding a statue icon on the board are filled. Awards 2-8 bonus tile draws from the bag depending on the specific statue. More valuable than pillars.
Triggered when all spaces surrounding a window icon on the board are filled. Awards 3-12 bonus tile draws from the bag. The most valuable surrounding bonus type.

General term for pillar, statue, and window bonuses. All work the same way: fill every space adjacent to the icon to trigger the bonus. Completing these early maximizes the value of drawn tiles over remaining rounds.

End-game bonus awarded for filling all 6 spaces of a single star. Each completed star earns a significant point bonus. Requires a total of 21 tiles of that star's color over the course of the game (1+2+3+4+5+6).
End-game bonus awarded for filling the same number across all 6 stars. For example, filling every 1-space on all 6 stars completes the 1-number set. Lower numbers are cheaper to complete.

When a tile is placed, it scores points equal to the connected group size it joins. An isolated tile = 1 pt. A tile joining a group of 3 = 4 pts (new group size). This is the primary in-game scoring mechanism.
The first phase of each round where players take turns drafting tiles from factory displays and the center. Continues until all tiles have been drafted. Also called the drafting phase.

The second phase of each round where players simultaneously place tiles from their supply onto their star boards. Tiles are placed by paying the space's numbered cost. Surrounding bonuses are triggered during this phase.

The third phase of each round. Advance the round marker to reveal the new wild color, then refill all factory displays with 4 tiles each from the bag. The excess tile rule (max 4 kept) is enforced before this phase.

At the end of each round's Play phase, you may keep a maximum of 4 tiles in your supply. Any excess must be returned to the bag. Strategic tile selection for carry-over is critical for round-to-round planning.
